text: The concept of dependent origination — paticca-samuppada — gives anicca its mechanical precision. Nothing arises independently; every phenomenon, including a distracting thought, depends on a chain of antecedent conditions. Nagarjuna, the second-century Madhyamaka philosopher, argued in the Mulamadhyamakakarika that because all phenomena are dependently arisen, none possess inherent, stable existence. A distracting thought about tomorrow's deadline exists because anxiety, anticipation, and sensory cues converged. When those conditions shift — when attention is gently redirected — the thought loses its purchase. This is not willpower; it is an understanding of how phenomena structurally cannot persist without continual conditioning.
question: According to the passage, why does a distracting thought lose its hold when attention is redirected?

options.text: Willpower overrides the thought's emotional charge.
The thought has reached its natural duration limit.
Nagarjuna taught that redirection severs the self from the thought.
The thought cannot persist without continual conditioning.
